howItWorks.intro: The BMR calculator uses the Mifflin-St Jeor equation, considered the most accurate formula for estimating basal metabolic rate.
rules: Men: BMR = (10 × weight in kg) + (6.25 × height in cm) − (5 × age) + 5
Women: BMR = (10 × weight in kg) + (6.25 × height in cm) − (5 × age) − 161
BMR represents the calories your body burns at complete rest — just to maintain basic life functions
Multiply BMR by an activity factor (1.2–1.9) to estimate total daily energy expenditure (TDEE)
howItWorks.outro: Your BMR accounts for about 60-75% of your total daily calorie burn. The remaining comes from physical activity (15-30%) and the thermic effect of food (about 10%).

faq.question: What is the difference between BMR and TDEE?
faq.answer: BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) is the calories your body burns at complete rest — just breathing, circulating blood, and maintaining organs. TDEE (Total Daily Energy Expenditure) includes BMR plus calories burned through physical activity and digestion.

faq.question: Why is Mifflin-St Jeor more accurate than Harris-Benedict?
faq.answer: The Mifflin-St Jeor equation was developed in 1990 using modern data and has been shown in multiple studies to predict BMR within 10% for most people. The older Harris-Benedict equation (1919) tends to overestimate BMR, especially for overweight individuals.

faq.question: How can I use BMR for weight loss?
faq.answer: To lose weight, you need to eat fewer calories than your TDEE (BMR × activity factor). A deficit of 500 calories per day leads to about 1 lb of weight loss per week. Never eat below your BMR, as this can slow your metabolism and be unhealthy.

faq.question: Does muscle mass affect BMR?
faq.answer: Yes, muscle tissue burns more calories at rest than fat tissue. Each pound of muscle burns about 6 calories per day at rest, compared to 2 calories per pound of fat. This is why strength training can increase your BMR over time.

faq.question: Does BMR change with age?
faq.answer: Yes, BMR decreases by about 1-2% per decade after age 20, primarily due to loss of muscle mass and hormonal changes. This is why maintaining muscle through strength training becomes increasingly important with age.

faq.question: What activity multipliers should I use?
faq.answer: Sedentary (desk job, little exercise): BMR × 1.2. Lightly active (1-3 days/week): BMR × 1.375. Moderately active (3-5 days/week): BMR × 1.55. Very active (6-7 days/week): BMR × 1.725. Extremely active (athlete): BMR × 1.9.
